coredata.dc:title: Surface State-Based panchromatic luminescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have shown a broad application prospect in the fields of sensing and detection, biological imaging, and optoelectronic devices. However, it is still challenging to adopt a simple and green synthesis route and to develop new precursor systems to prepare full-color luminescent carbon dots. This study proposes a mechanism for fine regulation of carbon dot fluorescence spectra based on surface states of CN, COC, and OH, among which CN play a major role in long wavelength emission while COC and OH are responsible for the blue shift of emission wavelength. Using 4,4-bipyridine and p-phenylenediamine as precursors in safe and environmentally friendly glycol and water as solvents for the first time, the fine spectral carbon dots with full spectrum luminescence from purple (441 nm) to red (627 nm) were successfully synthesized by simply changing the composition of the reaction solvent and using a short reaction time. Compared with other reports on regulating polychromatic carbon dots, our method is more refined and has a wider distribution of luminescent colors. In addition, the obtained carbon dots based on such surface state luminescence mechanism have shown good application prospects in specific detection of Fe3+and cell labeling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Detection of ions and cell labeling
Green chemistry
Luminescence
Panchromatic luminescent carbon dots
Surface state

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of The Multicolour fluorescent carbon dots
The R-CQDs applied to detect Fe3+
The Exploration of the luminescence mechanism of carbon dots
The Multi-Color tuning mechanism of carbon dots
The Verification of the luminescence mechanism of carbon dots and Applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis, luminescence mechanism and application of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots.

Synthetic strategy of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots. (A) Synthesis of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots using p-phenylenediamine and 4,4′-bipyridine as raw materials by solvothermal method. (B) Photographs of carbon dots under daylight (top) and UV light (excited at 365 nm) (bottom). (C) Normalized PL emission spectra of all carbon dots at 365 nm excitation.

The morphological characterizations and compositional analysis of selected carbon dots. TEM images and particle size distribution diagrams (inset) of (A) R-CQDs and (C) G-CQDs. HRTEM of (B) R-CQDs and (D) G-CQDs. (E) Band gap energy of R-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs and B-CQDs. (F) The full XPS spectra of R-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs and B-CQDs. (G) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s of R-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs and B-CQDs.
The compositional analysis of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs.

Optical properties and structural characterizations of selected carbon dots. Absorption spectra and PL emission spectra of (A) P-CQDs, (B) B-CQDs, (C) G-CQDs, (D) Y-CQDs, (E) O-CQDs, and (F) R-CQDs under excitation of different wavelengths of light. (G) PL emission spectra of R-CQDs as a function of concentration. (H) FTIR spectra of carbon dots. (I) Raman spectra of R-CQDs, O-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs, B-CQDs, and P-CQDs.

Carbon dots based on surface state luminescence mechanism can be used for specific detection of Fe3+. (A) PL emission spectra of R-CQDs when adding an equal volume of Fe3+ ion solution with a concentration of 0–300 μM. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. Inset shows the linearity of F/F0 against the measured Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 0–120 μM). (C) fluorescence images of R-CQDs at 365 nm excitation after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (D) The effect of other interfering metal ions on fluorescence intensity of carbon dots (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively). (E) fluorescence images of (D) at 365 nm excitation of R-CQDs after adding different metal ions. (F) UV–vis absorption spectrum, and (G) fluorescence lifetime of R-CQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions.

Multi-colored carbon dots can be used for labeling imaging of Hela cells and R-CQDs can be used for intracellular visualization of detecting Fe3+. Fluorescence microscopic images of Hela cells incubated with (A) Y-CQDs, (B) O-CQDs and (C) R-CQDs, respectively (Fluorescence images of (A-B) were captured at 488 nm excitation and (C) at 552 nm excitation, respectively. Bright field (left), dark field (middle) and composite field (right)). (I-V) images of Hela cells at iron ion concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 200 and 300 μM, respectively. To verify the real-time quenching effect of iron ions on fluorescent carbon dots, we adjusted the concentration of iron ions in the cell sample by gradually increasing the amount of iron ions to the cell sample, and observed their dynamic quenching at different concentrations (0, 50 μ M, 100 μ M, 200 μ M, 300 μ M). Their bright fields are the same with that one at concentration 0 (C).

Multi-colored carbon dots can be used for labeling imaging of L929 cells and R-CQDs can be used for intracellular visualization of detecting Fe3+. Fluorescence microscopic images of L929 cells incubated with (A) G-CQDs, (B) Y-CQDs, (C) O-CQDs, (D) R-CQDs, respectively (Fluorescence images of (A-C) were captured at 488 nm excitation and (D) at 552 nm excitation, respectively. Bright field (left), dark field (middle) and composite field (right)). (I-V) images of L929 cells at iron ion concentrations of 0, 50, 100, 200 and 300 μM, respectively. To verify the real-time quenching effect of iron ions on fluorescent carbon dots, we adjusted the concentration of iron ions in the cell sample by gradually increasing the amount of iron ions to the cell sample, and observed their dynamic quenching at different concentrations (0, 50 μ M, 100 μ M, 200 μ M, 300 μ M). Their bright fields are the same with that one at concentration 0 (D).
The C, N, O element content, oxygen/carbon and nitrogen/carbon atom ratio of R-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs and B-CQDs.
XPS data analyses of the C1s and N 1 s spectra of R-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs and B-CQDs.
Quantum yields of carbon dots under their optimal excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Surface State-Based panchromatic luminescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Advance Technologies of Materials, Ministry of Education, College of Medicine and School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Jiaotong University, Chengdu 610031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Advance Technologies of Materials
College of Medicine and School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, College of Basic and Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, College of Basic and Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have shown a broad application prospect in the fields of sensing and detection, biological imaging, and optoelectronic devices. However, it is still challenging to adopt a simple and green synthesis route and to develop new precursor systems to prepare full-color luminescent carbon dots. This study proposes a mechanism for fine regulation of carbon dot fluorescence spectra based on surface states of CN, COC, and OH, among which CN play a major role in long wavelength emission while COC and OH are responsible for the blue shift of emission wavelength. Using 4,4-bipyridine and p-phenylenediamine as precursors in safe and environmentally friendly glycol and water as solvents for the first time, the fine spectral carbon dots with full spectrum luminescence from purple (441 nm) to red (627 nm) were successfully synthesized by simply changing the composition of the reaction solvent and using a short reaction time. Compared with other reports on regulating polychromatic carbon dots, our method is more refined and has a wider distribution of luminescent colors. In addition, the obtained carbon dots based on such surface state luminescence mechanism have shown good application prospects in specific detection of Fe3+and cell labeling.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Detection of ions and cell labeling

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have become a research hotspot in nanotechnology in recent years, and they are widely employed in bioimaging, fluorescence sensing, LEDs, and other fields due to their good biocompatibility, fluorescence stability, and customizable luminescence qualities.[1–7] However, most of the reports focus on the synthesis of carbon dots with short-wavelength or monochromatic blue or green fluorescence, which severely limits their application in bioimaging and luminous devices.[8,9] Despite the fact that there have been more studies on multicolor carbon dots, their preparation processes still have some shortcomings that need to be addressed. Wang et al. modified the composition and structure of carbon dots by using various carbon sources and surface modifications.[10] Or, Li et al. and Wang et al. changed the synthesis conditions (e.g., reaction temperature, time, pH, and so on) to control the luminous properties of carbon dots.[11,12] Holá et al. used silica gel column chromatography to create polychromatic carbon dots.[13] However, such approaches frequently necessitate complex synthesis procedures, long reaction times, and precise chemical modulation, resulting in high costs, and meanwhile it is still difficult for accurate control of the luminescence mechanism of multicolor carbon dots and fine modulation of the luminescence color. Furthermore, many reports on regulating the fluorescence color of carbon dots through solvents mostly use hazardous chemicals such as flammable, explosive, strong acids, and strong bases as reaction solvents, which have low safety factors and the regulated multicolor fluorescence is not precise enough to cover the entire visible spectrum.[14,15] Therefore, selecting safe and environmentally friendly raw materials and using a simple synthesis route to prepare carbon dots that can achieve full spectrum luminescence at a single excitation wavelength is of great significance.

The luminescent mechanisms of carbon dots typically include quantum size effects, graphite nitrogen, surface states, and bandgap transitions.[11,13,14,16–18] Currently, to achieve multicolor luminescent carbon dots often requires the assistance of the synergistic effect of two or more of these mechanisms. The regulatory mechanism is relatively complex, and the luminescence colors of the obtained carbon dot are not rich and fine enough. There are few reports on relying solely on a single mechanism to regulate the production of multi-color luminescent carbon dots with much finer luminescent colors. Therefore, using a single luminescence regulation mechanism to prepare more finely luminescent panchromatic carbon dots under reliable and mild conditions remains a huge challenge.

In view of this, this study would adopt a green and environmentally friendly strategy, select glycol, water safe and environmentally friendly solvents, and affect the interaction between alcohol solvents and carbon dot surfaces by changing their chain length, polarity and protonation, so as to regulate the surface structure of carbon dots, and meanwhile, by introducing strong electron withdrawing groups and strong electron donor groups on the multi ring structure, and using their strong conjugation, to finally achieve fine regulation of the fluorescence properties of carbon dots. Therefore, based on the multi ring structure system, we have opened up a new way to prepare multicolor carbon dots using an innovative material combination of 4,4-bipyridine and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. At the same time, we have proposed a mechanism to achieve panchromatic luminescence with fine spectrum by controlling the surface state of carbon dots, where surface defect state and surface functional groups CN are responsible for the red shift of emission wavelength, while COC and OH are responsible for the blue shift of emission wavelength. The prepared multicolor carbon dots exhibit sensitive and specific Fe3+ detection capabilities, as well as the ability to label cells with multiple colors (Fig. 1 ). This study can provide useful references for the research and development of multicolored carbon dots.

P-Phenylenediamine was purchased from the Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4,4′-Bipyridine was acquired from Alpha Chemical Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). ethylene glycol, 1,2-Propanediol, Diethylene glycol, triethylene glycol, phosphoric acid and acetic acid were obtained from Kolon Chemicals Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Thiazolyl blue (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich(St. Louis, MO, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of The Multicolour fluorescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots ranging from purple to red luminescence were prepared from p-phenylenediamine and 4,4′-bipyridine in different solvents by means of a one-step solvothermal method, respectively. Firstly, p-phenylenediamine (0.05 g), 4,4′-bipyridine (0.05 g) were added in the solvent and stirred for 30 min to the solution. Then, it was transferred into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave in an oven and heated at 200 ℃ for 3 h, followed by natural cooling to room temperature. Subsequently, the obtained carbon dot solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove large particles, and then put into a 500 Da dialysis bag for dialysis using deionized water (RO water) as the dialysate for 24 h. The dialysated carbon dot solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for a second time. Finally, the filtered solution was poured into a petri dish and freeze-dried in a freeze-dryer for 24 h to obtain CDs powder. The purple to red luminescent carbon dots were prepared in triethylene glycol (20 mL), diethylene glycol (20 mL), ethylene glycol (20 mL) / acetic acid (500 μL), 1,2-propanediol (20 mL) / phosphoric acid (90 μL), ethylene glycol (20 mL), and ethylene glycol/ water with a ratio of 2:1 (20 mL) solvents, respectively. These fluorescent carbon dots were labeled as P-CQDs, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs, respectively.

The TEM images of carbon dots were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope. The XRD patterns were performed on a Rigaku Ultma IV X-ray diffractometer. The UV–vis spectra and the optical diffuse reflectance spectra were measured on a UV-2802 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The band gap of the carbon dots was calculated by the equation:
(Î±hÎ½)
2
K
=hÎ½
-E g
, (where α is the absorbance of the sample, h is Planck's constant (6.625 × 10-34 J∙s), ν is the photon frequency (s−1), K is a constant and Eg is the bandgap (eV))[19,20]. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a F98 spectrofluorometer. The FTIR spectra were obtained on a 5700 Infrared spectrometer. The XPS spectra were measured by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Al/Ka as the source. The Raman spectra were performed on a XplorRA ONE Raman Microscope excited with 532 nm laser. The fluorescence lifetime testing was recorded using FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer. Cellular fluorescence images were captured with a IX-51 fluorescence microscope.

Quantum yields (QY) of fluorescent carbon dots were determined by a relative method.[53,54] In this experiment, quinine sulfate, rhodamine 6G and rhodamine B were used as standard references, where the quantum yields of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 were 54 %, rhodamine 6G and rhodamine B in anhydrous ethanol were 94 % and 56 %, respectively. The equation for calculating the quantum yield is as follows:
(1)
ϕ x
=
ϕ r
×
A r
A x
×
I x
I r
×
η
x
2
η
r
2
where ϕ is the quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the selected excitation wavelength, I is the fluorescence integrated area, η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts x and r refer to the substance to be measured and the standard reference, respectively. In order to minimize re-absorption effects absorbencies, the absorbance of the measured standard reference and all samples to be measured were less than 0.05.

Fluorescence Stability Experiments: To investigate the fluorescence stability of carbon dots, firstly, 400 μL of CDs were added to 400 μL of PBS with different pH values (2–13), and the fluorescence emission spectra were measured under the optimal excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength was recorded to investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Then, 400 μL of CDs were added to 400 μL of sodium chloride solutions with different concentrations (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mol/L) to investigate the salt tolerance of CDs. Subsequently, 400 μL of CDs were added to 400 μL of hydrogen peroxide solutions with different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 mmol/L) to investigate the antioxidant properties of CDs. Finally, the CDs were continuously irradiated under the excitation light for 1 h. The changes of fluorescence intensity were measured and recorded every ten minutes to investigate the photobleaching resistance of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The R-CQDs applied to detect Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Sensitivity detection of Fe3+: After diluting the R-CQDs ethanol solution, 16 portions of 750 μL of R-CQDs solution (5 mg/mL) were taken to be added in 16 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes, followed by adding 750 μL of FeCl3 solutions with concentrations of 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300 μmol/L to the above 16 R-CQDs solutions, mixing, measuring the fluorescence spectra and calculating the detection limits.

Selective detection of Fe3+: After diluting the R-CQDs ethanol solution, 21 portions of 750 μL of R-CQDs ethanol solutions (5 mg/mL) were taken to be added in 21 portions of 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes, followed by adding 750 μL of aluminum chloride, copper sulfate, barium chloride, calcium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium chloride, ferrous sulfate, lead chloride, nickel chloride, zinc chloride, iron chloride, silver sulfate, strontium chloride, cadmium sulfate, mercury chloride, manganese chloride, lithium chloride, stannous chloride, cobalt acetate solution and RO water at a concentration of 3 mmol/mL to the above 21 R-CQDs solutions, mixing, and then measuring their fluorescence spectra.

In this experiment, human cervical cancer cells Hela, fibroblasts L929, and macrophages Raw264.7 were used as samples. First, the three kinds of cells were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well, and then incubated in a cell culture incubator at 37℃ for 24 h to make the cells grow against the wall, Next, R-CQDs, O-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs, B-CQDs and P-CQDs at a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL were added and continued to be cultured for 3 days to test the cytotoxicity of carbon dots using the MTT method, while the cell imaging experiments were performed after incubating for 4 h. After removing the medium, the well plates were washed with PBS twice and 200 μL of PBS was added to each well to submerge the cells. Finally, the well plates were placed under a fluorescent microscope for cell imaging. In addition, to investigate the sensing effect of R-CQDs on intracellular Fe3+ ions, we adjusted the concentration of iron ions in the cell sample by gradually increasing the amount of iron ions to the cell sample, and observed their dynamic quenching at different concentrations (0, 50 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM). Their bright fields are the same with that one at concentration 0.

In this study, each experimental part was repeated three times, and the results of each study were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Students-test was applied to compare between two groups, and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for statistical tests for comparison between multiple groups. When * p < 0.05, the difference between the two groups was considered statistically significant, ** P<0.01 and *** p < 0.001 indicate very significant differences between the two groups.

The luminescence mechanism of carbon dots includes band gap transition of conjugated π-domain (molecular state), surface defect state, graphite nitrogen and quantum size effect.[21–24] Although there have been some reports on the regulation of multicolor carbon dots through surface states, the regulation mechanism is not clear and the color regulation is not refined enough. Therefore, a synthesis strategy of panchromatic luminescent carbon dots based on surface state was proposed in this study. Considering that the strong conjugation between the strong electron donor and electron acceptor groups on the polycyclic structure is conducive to the luminescence emission of long wavelength of carbon dots, we chose 4,4-bipyridine and p-phenylenediamine as the ideal precursor combination. Based on the strong conjugation between them and their interaction with the polyol solvent, carbon dots that can emit long wavelengths (such as red and orange fluorescence) were first synthesized. Then, in view of that the solvent is an effective means of regulation, by increasing the chain length and polarity of the reaction solvent, more OH and COC groups were endowed to the carbon dots to synthesize blue and purple fluorescent carbon dots. On this basis, we adopted the idea of reverse regulation and synthesized green and yellow fluorescent carbon dots in solvents with small chain length and polarity by increasing protonation process. The results show that our strategy can successfully synthesize PL-based fluorescent carbon dots only by adjusting surface states and using a short reaction time. As shown in Fig. 2
A, these fluorescent carbon dots are labeled as P-CQDs, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs. In order to rule out the possibility of luminescence emissions caused by quantum size effect, graphite nitrogen and band gap factors, we conducted a series of relevant characterizations for the obtained carbon dots and evaluated them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Exploration of the luminescence mechanism of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
To exclude the effect of quantum size effect, the morphology and size of the carbon dots were characterized using transmission electron microscopy. It can be seen that the carbon dots are quasi spherical and well dispersed. The average particle sizes of R-CQDs and G-CQDs are 3.85 nm and 2.89 nm (Fig. 3
A, C), respectively, with a particle size difference of only 0.96 nm. In addition, as shown in Figure S1, the average particle sizes of P-CQDs, B-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and O-CQDs are 2.70 nm, 2.75 nm, 3.36 nm, and 3.62 nm, respectively. The maxim particle size difference was observed between R-CQDs and P-CQDs which only is 1.15 nm. Carbon dots with the multicolor luminescence that is typically created by the quantum size effect have a large range of size changes. For example, green and red luminescent carbon dots prepared by Wu et al. have a size difference of 3.58 nm.[25] Similarly, Ding et al.'s green and red luminescent carbon dots also have a size difference of 4.5 nm.[14] Both of which exhibit significant quantum size effects leading to different fluorescence emissions. Therefore, compared with them, the quantum size effect is not the main mechanism for the multicolor luminescence emission of carbon dots in this study. In addition, high-resolution TEM images (Fig. 3B, D) show that both R-CQDs and G-CQDs exhibit lattice fringes with crystal plane spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.27 nm, respectively, attributed to the (002) and (020) crystal planes of graphite. Meanwhile, X-ray diffraction (Figure S2) also demonstrated that R-CQDs exhibit typical graphite structural characteristics due to the stacking synthesis of conjugated aromatic hydrocarbon systems. The band gap changes of the sample were tested by UV visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy, which ruled out the influence of band gap factors on carbon dot luminescence. As shown in Fig. 3E, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs all have absorption in the range of 200 to 300 nm. The band gaps were calculated to be 4.4, 4.2, 4.6, and 4.8 eV, respectively, showing the band gap changes were not significant from blue luminescent carbon dots to luminescent carbon dots. The reported luminescence induced by the bandgap transition of carbon dots has a bandgap difference of 1.45 eV from blue luminescence to red luminescence.[11] These results indicate that the internal structure and quantum size affected by the bandgap changes of carbon dots have little effect on their multicolor fluorescence properties.[26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Multi-Color tuning mechanism of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
To confirm how the surface state of the carbon dots affects the short wavelength emission of the carbon dots, we demonstrated the PL blueshift induced by modulating the amount of OH and COC through varying solvent chain length and polarity. The surface structural composition of the carbon dots was investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 5
H) show that the prepared carbon dots possessed abundant polar functional groups, such as OH/NH (3500–3100 cm−1), CO (1700–1600 cm−1) and COC (1200–1090 cm−1), ensuring good solubility in polar organic solvents. In addition, CH (2900–2800 cm−1), CO/CN (1750–1600 cm−1), CC (1530–1450 cm−1), and CN (1400–1350 cm−1) groups were also observed, indicating the formation of polyaromatic structures in the carbon dots during the solvothermal process. From the infrared spectra of R-CQDs to P-CQDs, it can be observed that both the OH/NH stretching vibration (3500 to 3100 cm−1) and the COC stretching vibration (1200 to 1090 cm−1) show an enhanced trend. Generally, the wider OH absorption band indicates the multiple structures of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots,[28] thus verifying the main role of OH and COC in inducing PL blue shift.

Due to the dominant role of surface states in the long wavelength emission of carbon dots, it has also been demonstrated in the UV absorption spectrum that the emission mechanism of carbon dots is related to surface states, and surface defect state and surface functional group CN are responsible for long wavelength emission. In the UV region (Fig. 5A-F), they all exhibit absorption in the 200–300 nm range, which corresponds to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon cores.[29,30] However, in the low-energy region, P-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs and R-CQDs exhibit different absorption bands at 529 nm, 354 nm, 521 nm and 345 nm, respectively. These are attributed to n-π* transitions of the CN bond,[31,32] and the positions of the different absorption bands indicate that they have different surface states, which is similar to many other reported CDs.[33,34] In particular, P-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs all display absorption peaks in the range of 500 nm-600 nm, which is due to certain surface states capturing excited state energy. This phenomenon is related to surface defect state.[35] The effect of the degree of defect on the PL redshift was further demonstrated by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5I, all R-CQDs, O-CQDs, Y-CQDs, G-CQDs, B-CQDs and P-CQDs exhibit two characteristic peaks at 1354 cm−1 and 1547 cm−1, corresponding to the d-band and G-band of carbon material, respectively, where the d-band suggests a disordered structure or defect and the G-band suggests a sp2 graphitic structure. This implies that there is disorder and sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms.[36–39] The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) usually implies the degree of structural defects and graphitization in carbon dots.[15,40] ID/IG values of P-CQDs, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs and R-CQDs are 0.55, 0.61, 0.77, 0.79, 0.82 and 0.86respectively, indicating that the degree of defects gradually increases from P-CQDs to R-CQDs. In summary, the long-wavelength luminescence mechanism of carbon dots is attributed to the surface defect state and CN plays a major role in the redshift for emission wavelength.

In this study, we demonstrated that the carbon dots can be finely tuned for luminescence throughout the visible spectral range based on the surface-state luminescence mechanism. To determine the emission wavelength and fluorescence properties of different carbon dots, we further tested their fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 5A-F, P-CQDs, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, and Y-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL emissions, while O-CQDs and R-CQDs exhibit excitation-independent PL emissions. This excitation-independent PL of the carbon dots is mainly attributed to their surface states.[41] In particular, P-CQDs have a dual-emission fluorescence property with emission peaks at 441 nm and 600 nm, respectively. The maximum emission peaks of P-CQDs, B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs and R-CQDs are at 441, 467, 483, 547, 603 and 627 nm, respectively, corresponding to violet, blue, green, yellow, orange and red fluorescence, respectively (Table 3 ). This verifies that we have precisely tuned the fluorescent carbon dots with tunable PL emissions. Their corresponding quantum yields were shown in Table 3. Carbon dots may also exhibit concentration-dependent PL emission properties, as shown in Fig. 5G, the emission wavelength of R-CQDs shifted slightly towards red with increasing concentration, and there was a maximum emission wavelength of 627 nm when the concentration of R-CQDs was 667 μg/mL. Compared with the carbon dots reported in literature,[42] the fluorescence properties of the carbon dots in this study were more stable and exhibited a slight concentration-dependent behavior. This is due to with the increase of concentration, the energy gap of the carbon dots in the cluster becomes smaller due to the expanded electron conjugation of the interaction energy of the carbon dots, while the energy transfer between the isolated carbon dots causes greater energy attenuation, which leads to a redshift of aggregation-induced emission and a decrease in their efficiency of carbon dots.[43].

To further demonstrate the good fluorescence stability of the prepared carbon dots, we investigated the effects of external factors (such as different pH levels, concentrations of NaCl and H2O2, and laser radiation time) on the fluorescence intensity of R-CQDs. As shown in Figure S3, no significant increase or decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed within the pH range of 2 to 13. Even when R-CQDs were in a medium environment of 2 mol/L NaCl or 0.1 mmol/L H2O2, their fluorescence intensity hardly changes significantly. Additionally, even though the R-CQDs were continuously exposed to an Xe lamp for one hour, their fluorescence intensity remained constant. The results reveal that the obtained R-CQDs in this study have good fluorescence stability, implying their potential for application in various complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Verification of the luminescence mechanism of carbon dots and Applications
ce:section.ce:para:
To further verify that the luminescence properties of our prepared carbon dots are dominated by surface state, we designed metal ion-induced fluorescence quenching experiments. Usually, carbon dots with luminescence properties dominated by surface states have surface functional groups and defect-controlled luminescence sites that are more prone to interact with Fe3+, for example, the occurrence of energy transfer resulting in fluorescence quenching.[44–46] As shown in Fig. 6
A-C, the results showed a gradual quenching of fluorescence as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 0 to 300 μM, verifying that the fluorescence quenching was due to the effect of the surface state of R-CQDs. Furthermore, when the concentration of Fe3+ was in the range of 0 to120 μM, there was a good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 of R-CQDs and Fe3+ concentration. The fitted linear curve is
F/
F 0
= -0.00427c
Fe
3+
+ 0.99937
. Based on the equation 3S0/K (where S0 is the standard deviation of the blank sample and K is the slope of the linear fit curve), the detection limit was calculated to be 0.24 μM, indicating a high potential for detecting Fe3+ content (5.4 μM) in drinking water.[47] This method for detecting Fe3+ based on long-wavelength emission of red fluorescent carbon dots in this study is comparable to or even superior to those already reported in the literature.[47–50] Further selective experiments were conducted on R-CQDs for detecting Fe3+. The results demonstrate that even with the presence of other interfering metal ions, it still exhibits good specificity and anti-interference ability.

To better understand how R-CQDs based on the surface-state luminescence mechanism detect Fe3+, we tested the changes of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes before and after adding Fe3+ to R-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 6F, the position of the absorption peak of the carbon dots did not change before and after the addition of Fe3+, however, only the intensity decreased. While, in the fluorescence lifetime test (Fig. 6G), the average lifetime of R-CQDs plummeted from 8.25 ns to 5.65 ns after adding Fe3+, resulting in a decay of its fluorescence lifetime by 32 %. Fluorescence quenching usually originates from dynamic or static quenching. Among them, dynamic quenching only affects the excited state of fluorescent molecules and does not change the absorption spectrum of fluorescent substances, but it shortens the fluorescence lifetime.[51,52] The above experimental results suggest that R-CQDs’ quenching for Fe3+ detection is a dynamic quenching process, the related results confirm the high rate and efficiency of transient fluorescence quenching caused by rapid nonradiative energy transfer between the metal ions and the electron or vibrational modes on the surface of the carbon dots, which leads to the transient quenching of fluorescence. Therefore, based on such surface-state luminescence mechanism of carbon dots, we have provided a fast and specific means to detect Fe3+.

The cytotoxicity test results of multi-color carbon dots show that they all have good cell compatibility（Figure S4）. In addition, the multi-colored carbon dots prepared in this study can also be used for labeling imaging of cells. As shown in Figs. 7-8
and Figure S5, the four types of carbon dots, R-CQDs, O-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and G-CQDs, all have good biocompatibility. Additionally, due to their small size and excellent tissue penetration ability, R-CQDs, O-CQDs and Y-CQDs can easily enter cells through Hela, L929 and Raw264.7 cell membranes, subsequently, these cells emit red, orange or yellow fluorescence, respectively. Interestingly, G-CQDs can label L929 and Raw264.7 normal cells, but cannot label Hela cancer cells. This may be due to the fact that green luminescent carbon dots have lower tissue penetration than red and orange luminescent ones, which may have great potential in distinguishing between normal cells and Hela cells. Additionally, R-CQDs are also capable of intracellular visualization of detecting Fe3+.

In this study, a surface-state based engineering strategy has been witnessed in the successful synthesis of panchromatic luminescent carbon dots by using a fixed precursor mixture of 4,4-bipyridine and p-phenylenediamine and only by tuning the chain length, polarity and protonation of polyol solvent. The obtained carbon dots based on the surface state luminescence mechanism exhibit stable fluorescence performance and can quickly and specifically detect Fe3+. In addition, they show good cell compatibility and multicolor cell labeling imaging ability. The idea of achieving full spectrum luminescence by controlling surface states proposed in this study will be beneficial for understanding the polychromatic emission mechanism of carbon dots, in order to obtain carbon dots with finer luminescence structure. At the same time, this study provides a simple, controllable, safe, and environmentally friendly strategy for the preparation of polychromatic fluorescent carbon dots, which can also bring new material selection and performance improvement to other fields such as electronic devices in the future.

Hangzhen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiafan Bai: Visualization, Investigation. Xiangli Chen: Investigation. Linyu Wang: Investigation. Wenzhen Peng: Resources. Yuancong Zhao: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jie Weng: Methodology, Conceptualization. Wei Zhi: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jianxin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
